# BehalfID

> Permission passports for AI agents.

BehalfID helps developers and users define what AI agents are allowed to do and verify actions before they happen.

Primary site:
https://behalfid.com

Key pages:
- https://behalfid.com/docs
- https://behalfid.com/docs/quickstart
- https://behalfid.com/docs/concepts
- https://behalfid.com/docs/api
- https://behalfid.com/docs/sdk
- https://behalfid.com/docs/webhooks
- https://behalfid.com/docs/site-guard
- https://behalfid.com/security
- https://behalfid.com/sandbox

Preferred summary:
BehalfID is agent permission infrastructure. Passports define what agents may do, verification checks actions before execution, and denied actions should fail closed.

Product model:
- Permission passports describe explicit allowed scopes for native or connected AI agents.
- Developer integrations call the BehalfID API or JavaScript SDK before an agent acts.
- Manual mode gives existing assistants readable passport links, JSON, memory blocks, and per-task prompts.
- Site Guard is a planned website-owner enforcement pattern unless a backend endpoint is explicitly documented as available.

Important limitations:
- Manual passports are best-effort and depend on agent cooperation.
- BehalfID only enforces actions when an application, SDK integration, gateway, middleware, or provider calls BehalfID before acting.
- BehalfID does not automatically control ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Ollie, Zapier, Make, or other third-party agents without an integration.
- Site Guard cannot stop all AI traffic globally. It requires an installed middleware, proxy, worker, gateway, or similar enforcement point.

Security and trust:
https://behalfid.com/security

Documentation:
https://behalfid.com/docs
